text: During laparoscopy, the surgeon confirms the presence of a large mass at the splenic flexure. There is no visible infiltration of the ligament of Treitz, allowing for consideration of a multivisceral resection.

The surgeon opens the mesentery of the splenic flexure, isolating, clipping, and dividing the left colic artery. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped and divided near the tail of the pancreas. The mesentery is lifted and detached from the left kidney, removing the genital fascia. The mesentery of the transverse colon is divided up to the colonic wall. The gastrocolic ligament is divided, and the omentum is transacted distal to the tumor. The transverse colon is divided using a linear stapler. Pancreatic infiltration is observed, leading to the isolation, clipping, and division of the splenic vein and artery.

The surgeon transects the tail of the pancreas using a linear stapler after dividing the splenic vessels. The descending colon is mobilized and divided distal from the tumor using a linear stapler. The spleen is separated from its ligament to complete the en bloc resection. The infiltration of the greater curvature of the stomach is evident, and the gastroepiploic vessels are transected, followed by division of the greater curvature using a linear stapler.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels, completing the resection. A suprapubic incision is made, and a wound protector is inserted. The specimen is removed using an endo bag to prevent spillage of cancer cells into the abdominal cavity. After removal, choroidal hemostasis is controlled, and choroidal perfusion of the transverse and descending colon is assessed using fluorescence angiography. A side-to-side colonic intracorporeal anastomosis is performed.
